votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, summing the ASCII values of uppercase characters using equivalent helper functions and matching the precondition and specification.
The Python implementation correctly translates the Coq logic, summing the ASCII values of uppercase characters in the string.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, summing the ASCII values of uppercase characters.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_uppercase checks if ASCII value is between 65 and 90 inclusive, sum_uppercase_ascii iterates through the string summing ASCII values of uppercase characters, and problem_66_spec checks if output equals digitSum_impl(s). All concrete examples would produce the same results.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. Both check if characters are uppercase (ASCII 65-90) and sum their ASCII values. The is_uppercase function correctly checks the range, sum_uppercase_ascii iterates through the string summing uppercase character codes, and problem_66_spec correctly checks if output equals digitSum_impl(s). The examples in the comments match the expected behavior.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_uppercase checks if ASCII value is between 65 and 90 inclusive, sum_uppercase_ascii iterates through the string summing ASCII values of uppercase characters, and digitSum_impl returns this sum. The precondition returns True and the spec checks equality with digitSum_impl. All concrete examples would produce the same results.
Both implementations sum ord(c) for characters with ASCII 65-90 and otherwise skip; precondition and spec match exactly.
Both sum the ASCII codes of uppercase A-Z characters in the input string, with matching precondition and spec.

Both sum the ASCII codes of uppercase letters (A-Z) and the spec requires equality with that result; the precondition is always true. Python’s iteration and ord checks (65–90) match Coq’s nat_of_ascii and bounds.
